name: Jomiro Eming
quote: "You do too many different things. No-one will know what you do. You need to find a niche."
bio:
Jomiro curates The Wrong List. He's a graphic designer, creative strategist, writer, and multi-hyphenate... which, depending on who you ask, is either too much or exactly right.

He grew up between Germany and South Africa, never quite fully either. That kind of childhood does something to you: you become a student of belonging. You learn to read rooms, to code-switch, to notice the gap between what people say and what they mean. You get obsessive about stories—not as entertainment, but as the technology people use to make sense of themselves and each other.

The languages followed from the same compulsion. He speaks four of them now, and what that's mostly taught him is how differently people think when they're inside their own tongue. It made him better at his job, which has always really been the same job across different industries: figure out what's actually true about something, then find a way to make it land.

He's spent a decade doing that across branding, editorial, experience design, and publishing. He's written two books. He's worked with founders, cultural institutions, athletic brands, and theatre companies—usually on the problem underneath the problem.

He's been told, more than once, that he does too much. Doing too much, however, has shown him that the only limit to human potential is the story we tell ourselves.

name: Vanessa Raath
tagline: Global Keynote Speaker & AI Talent Strategist
quote: "You'd be great if you just toned it down a bit."
bio:
Vanessa Raath has spent the better part of two decades knee-deep in recruitment. Before that, she was a teacher, a diving instructor in the Far East and East Africa, and the kind of person who'd happily relocate for the right adventure. Turns out, the talent industry was the longest adventure of all.

She founded The Talent Hunter in 2019 and has since trained 8,000+ recruiters across 100+ countries, five continents, and more time zones than her body has fully forgiven her for. She's a global keynote speaker, AI consultant, and talent sourcing strategist who still does the actual work. That last bit matters to her more than the title does.

Vanessa sits at the intersection of rigorous methodology and genuinely useful AI application. She's not here to sell hype or hand you a prompt template and call it transformation. She's here to help you think better, source smarter, and stay irreplaceably human in a world that keeps trying to automate the good stuff.

She calls herself a Subject Matter Enthusiast in AI. Not an expert. Nobody who's paying attention should be calling themselves that right now.

Based on the South African coast, occasionally photographing wildlife, always asking the uncomfortable questions. #UpYourHuman

name: Christi du Toit
quote: "You get too obsessed with weird hobbies."
bio:
Christi is a multidisciplinary graphic designer and commercial illustrator with a background rooted in alternative visual culture. Comic books, skateboard graphics, gig posters, album art, and anything and everything that felt a bit rough around the edges provided all the inspiration he could ever hope for. What started as a teenage fixation on figuring out how to create cool graphics quickly became a core skill and a lifelong habit of teaching himself how to make things, just to see if he could.

He tends to go all-in on whatever catches his interest. That means digging deep into tools, techniques, and styles, and sticking with them long enough to really understand how they work. Over time, that way of thinking has shaped how he approaches design as a whole. It's less about quick solutions and more about pulling ideas apart and building them back up in a more interesting and unexpected way.

He's been working professionally for over a decade since receiving his degree in visual communication, developing processes that balance structure with experimentation. His work often leans toward a hand-drawn feel, keeping some of the imperfections intact and humanising the final product rather than striving for an aesthetic that feels overly digital or machine-made.

These days, he works professionally with clients across the world, crafting bespoke illustration and design that still carries a bit of that original influence, even if only in the mindset and process.

name: Clark Marchese
quote: "Your dreams are too big to ever become true. You're delusional."
bio:
Clark grew up in Idaho, where the mountains that met his backyard stand a little too tall for most people there to look beyond them. He, however, did look beyond them; and now he lives in Paris -- which, for a kid from Idaho, is either the logical conclusion, proof of delusion, or a healthy mix of both.

He learned early that if he wanted something, he'd have to find his own way to it. He's the founder of Pine Forest Media, a digital ecosystem of science and environmental storytelling. He interviews experts and action-takers across disciplines, translates their work, and packages it into digital products that are accessible, entry-level yet nuanced, globally distributed, and remind us of our own collective agency. He also travels to and reports from international scientific and environmental policy conferences around the world -- not on a legacy media salary, but because he decided that was the job.

What he's really good at is building bridges between things one might not assume are connected. He is also really good at project management and reverse engineering a pathway toward whatever unachievable goal was his starting point. He also takes pride in having built something people want to be a part of. Coffee addict. Theatre kid. Gay.

name: Candice Grobler
quote: "You just can't leave things as they are, can you?"
bio:
Cands is a systems-savvy entrepreneur driven by building actually impactful communities, so businesses can actually connect with their audiences while providing a channel for meaningful and collaborative product feedback and improvement.

She was retrenched during Covid, told by a well-meaning manager that she was unemployable, and decided to build something anyway. As a neurodiverse human navigating today's world, she's realised that the world was NOT made in a way that is inclusive and sustainable. And she's on a mission to help a million people become self-employed while serving their communities. Because self-empowerment is the first step towards being able to build a new world that ACTUALLY works for all of us, and our planet.

Cands' talks are candid, inspiring, and will leave you itching to tackle your dreams.

name: Natasha Rajalingam
bio:
Natasha Rajalingam is a Brand & Marketing Strategist who helps ambitious, experience-led brands turn big ideas into clear, compelling and commercially effective strategy.

With a background spanning tourism, luxury travel and high-growth challenger brands, she works at the intersection of strategy, storytelling and execution, equally comfortable shaping direction as she is driving delivery.

Her approach is grounded in understanding before action. She doesn't jump straight to outputs; she digs into the purpose, the context and the bigger picture, unpacking what's worked, what hasn't, and why.

Known for her sharp thinking and no-fluff style, Natasha partners closely with founders and teams to challenge assumptions, cut through noise and build brands that actually mean something.

Because the best work doesn't come from quick answers -- it comes from asking the right questions, and refusing to settle for surface-level solutions.

name: Lauren Dixon-Paver
quote: "You're too silly, too loud, and too emotional. Just do it the way we've always done it."
bio:
Due to a tragic misreading of the 2001 cinematic masterpiece "Josie and the Pussycats", Lauren works in advertising.

As a Senior Social Art Director at Boundless, Lauren gets paid to be chronically online, make cool things and cause mischief.

Outside of the office, Lauren likes to be chronically online, make cool things and cause mischief, but without fiscal compensation.

name: Jason Schneider
quote: "You are too idealistic to be practical."
bio:
Jason was always told that he asked too many questions that didn't have answers. That he cared too much about things that didn't matter or weren't tangible. He hasn't stopped. He keeps asking why the world has to be this way and how we make it better from a systems approach, believing that how we do things is more important than the things we do.

He's lived in enough places, Oregon, Vietnam, Tonga, Italy, corners of the US that don't make sense together, to know that most of what we think of as "how things work" are just habits and systems that we're not questioning enough. Jason studied history and economics trying to understand how our world got to where it is and what tools we have to make it better.

He's tried to make impacts on these systems on local levels in many different roles. Jason's been a city councillor, a local and state government employee, a small business owner, and for the last several years, a consultant who gets hired to create productive discomfort in rooms where people have forgotten the meaning of being human, so that they relearn the value of collective decision-making and meaning making through connection.

name: Shea Karssing
tagline: Author, Writer, Serial Full-Time Job Avoider
quote: "You want to do too much. Find a job, focus, and work your way up."
bio:
Shea Karssing is a writer based in the KZN Midlands. She is the author of 'Freelance Like a Boss', South Africa's first go-to guide for freelancers.

After years of side-hustling alongside full-time jobs, Shea took the leap into full-time freelancing eight years ago and hasn't looked back. She's all about continually levelling up, refining her services, sharpening her skills, and helping others build successful, sustainable businesses.

Shea has a bachelor's degree in journalism from Rhodes University. She loves waking up every day, being her own boss, and wearing activewear exclusively.

name: Patrick Vogel
bio: Patrick Vogel. Hamburg. Archviz studio. Collectible figures hidden around the city. A few apps. Some AI content. One very patient wife. Has ADHD, which means 14 projects at once and somehow all of them are kind of working. Doctors are baffled. So is he. Wakes up at 5am not because he's a morning person but because the day runs out too fast otherwise. Genuinely believes that the dumbest ideas are usually the best ones. Has enough evidence to be dangerous at dinner parties. Glad to be on this list. Slightly suspicious of anyone who isn't.

name: Kevin Lynch
quote: "You're too irreverent to be taken seriously."
bio:
Kevin Lynch is a Malmö, Sweden-based creative leader who most recently served as Creative Director at Oatly (the oat milk company, not the sunglasses one). While most of his career has been spent at creatively-driven advertising agencies in the US, Canada, and China, he's also served as the first director of marketing at China's oldest international school, Shanghai American School, and created side projects that attracted reactions ranging from adoration (Yearbnb) to death threats (Chicagoans for Rio).

Today, Kevin serves as the creative director for Belgian-based pet food company Edgard & Cooper. When he's not busy there, he runs The Wrong Agency, a creative consultancy that probably no one will ever hire.

name: Alley Marsh
quote: "You're too loud, too emotional, and care about your job too much."
bio:
She is a Talent and People Partner who believes work is never just about work — it's about how people feel, grow, and show up. She focuses on building cultures where there is true psychological safety, pushing the businesses she works in to be better than the status quo — to actually walk the talk of what they say they care about.

Her approach is intentionally human. She doesn't separate emotion from performance; she sees it as the driver of it. Where others might prioritise process, she pays attention to energy — what motivates people, what blocks them, and what makes teams actually thrive, not just function.

She has been told she is "too loud," but she uses her voice to challenge thinking and advocate for better. She has been told she is "too emotional," but that emotional intelligence is exactly what allows her to read a room, navigate complexity, and support people through change. She has been told she "cares too much" and "takes work too seriously," but for her, that care is the work — it's what creates environments where people do their best and most meaningful work.

She doesn't believe in toning it down. She believes in using it well.

name: Nicci Martin
quote: "You're a jack of all trades but a master of none."
bio:
Nicci Martin is a children's book illustrator who brings the cinematic eye of an animator and the strategic heart of an art director to the printed page. Nicci's creative journey has spanned motion design, advertising, and university lecture halls — a "polylingual" visual background that allows her to approach storytelling from every possible angle.

What sets Nicci apart is a deep, humble curiosity for the craft. Rather than sticking to one formula, she draws on a massive toolkit gathered from years of mastering visual communication to build worlds that feel both structurally sound and full of life. Nicci doesn't just draw scenes; she directs them, ensuring every character beat and composition serves the heart of the story.

Throughout Nicci's career, she was often called a "jack of all trades and master of none." But in the world of children's publishing where one must be a world-builder, a designer and a character actor all at once, she has found that being a lifelong student of everything creative is exactly what the story requires.

name: Amanda Perino
bio:
Amanda is a community builder, event planner, and proud overthinker who has somehow turned spiraling late-night thoughts into a full-time skill set, despite literally every friend, family member, mentor, and surf instructor telling her to "stop overthinking."

As the person behind the Rails Foundation, Amanda brings people together with a level of detail most would consider… excessive. But she considers: just right.

When she can, she surfs. What she lacks in skill, she makes up for in enthusiasm on the water.

name: Zandile Darko
tagline: Theatre Maker and Director of All Kinds of Creative Processes
quote: "You need to pick your niche, you cannot be everything at once."
bio:
Zandile Darko is an Afro-German theatre maker, actress, writer, and physical performer based in Hamburg. She has performed at venues including Kampnagel Hamburg, Münchner Kammerspiele, Staatsschauspiel Dresden, and Maxim Gorki Theater, and her work has been supported by the BKM Hamburg, Hamburgische Kulturstiftung, and Alfred Toepfer Stiftung.

She studied cultural sciences at Leuphana University of Lüneburg and the University of Ghana, then trained in devised physical theatre at LISPA (London International School of Performing Arts) on an Alfred Toepfer Foundation scholarship. She went on to complete an MFA in acting at Rose Bruford College in London and an MA in Performance Practices at ArtEZ University of the Arts in the Netherlands — both on scholarship from the German Academic Scholarship Foundation. From 2010 to 2018 she was a member of Hajusom, the award-winning transnational art project based in Hamburg, and from 2010 to 2024 a member of Label Noir, an Afro-German performance network of theatre and filmmakers in Berlin. She is also one half of the international duo Wet Paint, which she co-founded with author and theatre maker Therese Ramstedt and brought to the Edinburgh Festival Fringe.

In her artistic practice, she searches for strategies that make space for layered, nuanced, and complex stories, voices, and temporalities. Her work spans performance, mask play, video art, writing, and speculative fiction — often at the intersection of movement, memory, and postcolonial inquiry. Her bachelor's thesis asked to what extent performances can provide a critical counterstatement to official German colonial historiography — a thread that continues to run through her work today. Beyond the stage, she guides people to become aware of the patterns held in their bodies, helping them shift those patterns and move through life with more ease.
